error_type_examples.subject_verb_agreement: The group of students are going to the library.
error_type_examples.article: She adopted a old cat from the shelter.
error_type_examples.preposition: He is good in playing the guitar.
error_type_examples.verb_tense: Yesterday, she goes to the market and buys vegetables.
error_type_examples.spelling: The goverment announced new education policies.
error_type_examples.verb_form: He has went to the store three times this week.
error_type_examples.noun_number: She gave me a lot of advices about the project.
error_type_examples.pronoun: Everyone should bring their own lunch, but him forgot.
error_type_examples.word_order: She always is late to the morning meetings.
error_type_examples.punctuation: However she decided to stay and finish the report.
error_type_examples.conjunction: He studied hard, but however he failed the exam.
error_type_examples.adjective: This is the most cheapest option available in the store.

subject_verb_agreement.correct: The list of items was checked twice before shipping.
subject_verb_agreement.incorrect: The list of items were checked twice before shipping.

subject_verb_agreement.correct: Each of the students has submitted the assignment.
subject_verb_agreement.incorrect: Each of the students have submitted the assignment.

subject_verb_agreement.correct: Neither of the answers is correct.
subject_verb_agreement.incorrect: Neither of the answers are correct.

article.correct: She is an honest person who always tells the truth.
article.incorrect: She is a honest person who always tells the truth.

article.correct: I need an umbrella because it looks like rain.
article.incorrect: I need a umbrella because it looks like rain.

article.correct: The sun rises in the east every morning.
article.incorrect: A sun rises in the east every morning.

preposition.correct: She is very interested in classical music.
preposition.incorrect: She is very interested on classical music.

preposition.correct: He arrived at the station just in time.
preposition.incorrect: He arrived to the station just in time.

preposition.correct: They have been married for twenty years.
preposition.incorrect: They have been married since twenty years.

verb_tense.correct: By the time they arrived, she had already left.
verb_tense.incorrect: By the time they arrived, she already left.

verb_tense.correct: He has lived in this city for ten years.
verb_tense.incorrect: He is living in this city for ten years.

verb_tense.correct: I was watching TV when the phone rang.
verb_tense.incorrect: I watched TV when the phone rang.

spelling.correct: The committee approved the new budget unanimously.
spelling.incorrect: The commitee approved the new budget unanimously.

spelling.correct: She received a letter from her old friend.
spelling.incorrect: She recieved a letter from her old friend.

spelling.correct: The schedule for next week has been confirmed.
spelling.incorrect: The shedule for next week has been confirmed.

verb_form.correct: She has written three novels in the past decade.
verb_form.incorrect: She has wrote three novels in the past decade.

verb_form.correct: They were given extra time to complete the exam.
verb_form.incorrect: They were gave extra time to complete the exam.

verb_form.correct: He should have called before coming over.
verb_form.incorrect: He should have call before coming over.

noun_number.correct: The equipment was delivered on time.
noun_number.incorrect: The equipments were delivered on time.

noun_number.correct: She gave him some very useful feedback.
noun_number.incorrect: She gave him some very useful feedbacks.

noun_number.correct: The research shows several interesting results.
noun_number.incorrect: The researches show several interesting results.

pronoun.correct: Between you and me, this plan will not work.
pronoun.incorrect: Between you and I, this plan will not work.

pronoun.correct: The manager asked her to stay after the meeting.
pronoun.incorrect: The manager asked she to stay after the meeting.

pronoun.correct: Nobody knew where he had gone that evening.
pronoun.incorrect: Nobody knew where him had gone that evening.

word_order.correct: He hardly ever misses a deadline at work.
word_order.incorrect: He ever hardly misses a deadline at work.

word_order.correct: I have never seen such a beautiful landscape.
word_order.incorrect: I never have seen such a beautiful landscape.

word_order.correct: She always finishes her work before the deadline.
word_order.incorrect: She finishes always her work before the deadline.

punctuation.correct: After the meeting, she went straight home.
punctuation.incorrect: After the meeting she went straight home.

punctuation.correct: He bought apples, oranges, and bananas at the market.
punctuation.incorrect: He bought apples oranges and bananas at the market.

punctuation.correct: It's a difficult problem that requires careful thought.
punctuation.incorrect: Its a difficult problem that requires careful thought.

conjunction.correct: She was tired, so she went to bed early.
conjunction.incorrect: She was tired, but so she went to bed early.

conjunction.correct: He is tall, and his brother is quite short.
conjunction.incorrect: He is tall, and but his brother is quite short.

conjunction.correct: Although it was raining, they went for a walk.
conjunction.incorrect: Although but it was raining, they went for a walk.

adjective.correct: This is the most interesting book I have ever read.
adjective.incorrect: This is the most interestingest book I have ever read.

adjective.correct: She is more careful than her sister when driving.
adjective.incorrect: She is more carefuler than her sister when driving.

adjective.correct: He is the best player on the entire team.
adjective.incorrect: He is the most best player on the entire team.

subject_verb_agreement.input: The list of items were checked twice before shipping.
subject_verb_agreement.output: The collection of rare coins were sold at the auction.

article.input: She is a honest person who always tells the truth.
article.output: He found a interesting solution to the engineering problem.

preposition.input: She is very interested on classical music.
preposition.output: He is very good on solving complex mathematical equations.

verb_tense.input: By the time they arrived, she already left.
verb_tense.output: When I called him, he already ate his dinner.

spelling.input: The commitee approved the new budget unanimously.
spelling.output: The goverment introduced new regulations on food safety.

verb_form.input: She has wrote three novels in the past decade.
verb_form.output: They have saw this documentary at least three times.

noun_number.input: The equipments were delivered on time.
noun_number.output: She gave her colleagues some very useful advices.

pronoun.input: Between you and I, this plan will not work.
pronoun.output: The teacher asked I to present my findings first.

word_order.input: He ever hardly misses a deadline at work.
word_order.output: She always is late for the morning team meetings.

punctuation.input: After the meeting she went straight home.
punctuation.output: Before the concert they had dinner at a nearby restaurant.

conjunction.input: She was tired, but so she went to bed early.
conjunction.output: He was exhausted, and but he continued working until midnight.

adjective.input: This is the most interestingest book I have ever read.
adjective.output: That was the most cheapest meal on the entire menu.

prompt:
You are an expert linguist specializing in grammatical error generation. For the input sentence below:

  Step 1: Identify the grammatical error type (e.g. subject-verb agreement, article, preposition, verb tense, spelling).
  Step 2: Write a new, original sentence a B1-level foreign learner would produce on a different topic, exhibiting the same error type plus typical L1-transfer errors. The generated sentence MUST contain a grammatical error — do NOT produce a correct sentence.
  Step 3: Write the fully corrected version of the sentence from Step 2 (fix all grammatical errors so it reads as natural, fluent English).

Use this exact format:
Error type: <type>
Generated: <new sentence with error>
Ground truth: <corrected version of Generated>

Do NOT correct or rewrite the input sentence.

Input sentence: {sentence}
judge_prompt:
You are a GEC (grammatical error correction) quality evaluator. You receive an erroneous sentence and its proposed correction.

Assess two criteria:
  Redundancy: "trivial" if the error is too obvious or uninteresting for GEC evaluation (e.g. a single missing full-stop, one clearly wrong article in isolation, a trivial misspelling of one very common word with no other errors). "valid" otherwise.
  Correction: "correct" if the proposed correction is grammatically correct and natural English with no remaining errors. "incorrect" otherwise.

Use this exact format:
Redundancy: trivial/valid
Correction: correct/incorrect

Erroneous sentence: {sentence}
Proposed correction: {correction}

coedit.prefix: Fix the grammar of this sentence:
